assessment.support_text: Quote: "We conducted a single-center, randomized, double-blind (single-blinded for the dairy group), placebo-controlled trial for 21 wk to test the effects of calcium supplementation and dairy intake on body fat and serum lipids in obese Puerto Ricans." Comment: The report states blinding was double-blind but explicitly notes the dairy group was single-blinded, meaning participants/personnel could not be fully blinded to allocation for the dairy arm. Given primary outcomes (body fat, serum lipids, body weight) can be influenced by participant or personnel behaviours (dietary adherence, reporting, co-interventions) and the methods do not describe procedures to prevent unblinding of staff or participants in the dairy vs supplement/placebo comparisons, performance bias is likely. Outcome assessors for anthropometry were blinded, but that does not mitigate biased behaviours due to unblinded participants/personnel. Probably not done.

ground_truth.support_text: More than 40% of participants discontinued intervention in both groups during the 4-year study. We do not know how many participants were lost before the outcomes at 2 years (our outcome of interest), and how many after the first 2 years. Most participants (454/460) were included in the ITT analysis, but the study authors did not report for how many they managed to measure the outcome, and for how many they used the last observation carried forward ("mean of weeks 191 and 192 or last available value after randomization"). Apparently, the proportion of dropouts and the causes of dropout were equally distributed among the 2 groups.

ground_truth.domain: Selective reporting (reporting bias)
